title: What's Driving Rising Business Costs?

source.name: New York Fed Liberty Street Economics

summary: New York Fed regional business surveys show cost increases accelerated significantly in 2025, with service firms experiencing 7% increases (up from 5% in 2024) and manufacturers facing 8.5% increases (up from 5% in 2024). The sharpest cost increases were in employee health insurance (12.9% for services, 14.2% for manufacturers) and utilities (8.5% for both). Survey respondents expect costs to moderate to 5.4% for services and 4.8% for manufacturers in 2026.
key_points: Employee health insurance was the largest cost increase category at 12.9% for service firms and 14.2% for manufacturers; some firms reported increases of 25-50% at renewal
Utilities costs rose 8.5% for both types of firms, with about 15% of respondents reporting increases of 20% or more; AI-related data center growth cited as driver in some areas
Business insurance (liability, property, auto, workers comp) increased approximately 7% for service firms and 7.5% for manufacturers
Goods and materials costs climbed 8% for manufacturers vs 5.5% for service firms; tariffs on aluminum, steel, equipment, electrical supplies, auto parts, coffee, and cocoa cited as factors
Wage increases were relatively modest at 3.4% overall; rent increases were approximately 2%
Manufacturers reported larger cost increases than service firms across all major categories
why_it_matters: This regional survey provides timely evidence on business cost dynamics that inform inflation analysis. Rising health insurance and utilities costs—distinct from tariffs—suggest broad-based cost pressures beyond trade policy. The data implies potential margin compression for businesses and may influence Fed policy considerations around service sector inflation. Note: this is regional (NY-Northern NJ) rather than national data, and the authors explicitly state their views do not reflect the Federal Reserve's position.
macro_context: Regional business cost data suggests inflation pressures broadened in 2025 beyond goods prices to include services components like healthcare and utilities. Health insurance costs rising faster than wages (12.9-14.2% vs 3.4%) indicates non-labor cost pass-through may be dampening wage growth (examined in follow-up post). Expected moderation in 2026 (to 5.4% services, 4.8% manufacturers) aligns with 2024 pace. The authors note that tariffs contributed to goods/materials cost increases for manufacturers but represent only part of the overall cost story.

issues_found: The record does not include the source's explicit disclaimer that 'The views expressed in this post are those of the author(s) and do not necessarily reflect the position of the Federal Reserve Bank of New York or the Federal Reserve System.' While not a factual error, this is relevant context for interpreting the source's authority.

The topic field is set to 'macro catalysts' which is reasonable, though the notes correctly identify that the source is academic commentary rather than a market-moving catalyst. This is acceptable but worth noting.

notes: This is part one of a three-part series on business cost and price dynamics. The source is relevant to macro analysis (inflation/cost pressures) but is academic commentary rather than a market-moving catalyst. Not directly relevant to market structure (no discussion of market operations, plumbing, or trading infrastructure). Published 27 days before TODAY, making it recent. SOURCE METADATA listed topic as 'market structure' but actual content is about cost inflation dynamics—this classification may reflect the archive's tagging system rather than content alignment.
